mcqs.question: In a paging system, what is a frame?

options.text: A logical unit of memory allocation
A fixed-size block of physical memory
A variable-size block of virtual memory

mcqs.explanation: A frame is a fixed-size block in physical memory. It corresponds in size to a page in virtual memory, making page-to-frame mapping straightforward.

mcqs.question: Which allocation strategy gives each process a predetermined number of frames?
mcqs.explanation: Fixed allocation assigns a predetermined number of frames to each process at system design time, regardless of process size or priority.

mcqs.question: In proportional allocation, how are frames distributed?
mcqs.explanation: Proportional allocation distributes frames based on the size of each process. Larger processes receive proportionally more frames than smaller ones.

mcqs.question: Which type of allocation allows a process to take frames from other processes?
mcqs.explanation: Global allocation allows processes to compete for all available frames in the system, meaning a process can acquire frames previously allocated to other processes.

mcqs.question: What is thrashing?

options.text: Excessive CPU usage by a process
Spending more time in page I/O than execution

mcqs.explanation: Thrashing occurs when a process spends most of its time swapping pages in and out of memory rather than executing instructions, usually due to having too few frames.

mcqs.question: The minimum number of frames a process needs depends on:

options.text: The size of the CPU cache
The maximum pages referenced in a single instruction

mcqs.explanation: The minimum frames required equals the maximum number of pages that can be referenced by any single instruction, including the instruction itself and all its operands.

mcqs.question: What is the working set of a process?

options.text: All pages ever referenced by the process
The set of pages the process is currently using

mcqs.explanation: The working set is the collection of pages that a process is actively using at any given time. Keeping these pages in memory minimizes page faults.

mcqs.question: In priority allocation, which processes get more frames?
mcqs.explanation: Priority allocation gives more frames to higher-priority processes, allowing them to keep more pages in memory and execute faster than lower-priority processes.

mcqs.question: Page Fault Frequency (PFF) control is used for:
mcqs.explanation: PFF monitors the page fault rate of each process and dynamically adjusts the number of frames allocated to keep the fault rate within acceptable bounds.

mcqs.question: Which allocation type provides better memory protection?
mcqs.explanation: Local allocation provides better memory protection because each process can only use its own allocated frames and cannot interfere with other processes' memory.

mcqs.question: What happens to page fault rate as frames allocated increase?
mcqs.explanation: Initially, adding more frames significantly reduces page faults. After a point, additional frames have little benefit as the working set fits in memory.

mcqs.question: Local frame allocation means:

options.text: All frames are allocated from a local pool
A process can only use its own frames
Frames are allocated when needed locally
Allocation is done by the local CPU

mcqs.explanation: Local allocation restricts each process to using only the frames specifically allocated to it; it cannot take frames from other processes.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is NOT a frame allocation strategy?
mcqs.explanation: FIFO is a page replacement algorithm, not a frame allocation strategy. The allocation strategies determine how many frames each process gets.

mcqs.question: If total free frames = 100 and Process A has size 400KB out of total 1000KB, how many frames does Process A get in proportional allocation? (Assume frame size = 4KB)
mcqs.explanation: Process A's share = 400/1000 = 0.4. Frames = 0.4 × 100 = 40 frames. (Each frame is 4KB, so 400KB = 100 pages, but allocation is based on proportion of frames.)

mcqs.question: The main disadvantage of global allocation is:

options.text: A faulting process may steal frames from a higher-priority process

mcqs.explanation: In global allocation, a low-priority process experiencing thrashing can steal frames from a high-priority process, degrading the performance of the more important process.

mcqs.question: To prevent thrashing, the operating system must:

options.text: Allocate minimum frames to all processes
Ensure processes have enough frames for their working set

mcqs.explanation: Thrashing is prevented by ensuring each process has enough frames to hold its working set. If working sets fit in memory, page faults are minimized.

mcqs.question: What does the allocation of frames directly affect?

options.text: Page fault frequency and system performance

mcqs.explanation: Frame allocation directly impacts page fault frequency. Proper allocation reduces page faults and improves overall system performance.

mcqs.question: In a system with 50 free frames and 3 processes P1, P2, P3 with sizes 100, 200, 300 pages respectively, how many frames does P2 get in proportional allocation?
mcqs.explanation: Total size = 600. P2's proportion = 200/600 = 1/3. Frames = (1/3) × 50 ≈ 16.67, rounded to 15 or 16 frames.

mcqs.question: Which statement is TRUE about frame allocation?

options.text: More frames always reduce page faults linearly
Frame allocation has no impact on system performance
Global allocation offers more flexibility but less predictability
Local allocation always provides better performance

mcqs.explanation: Global allocation offers flexibility in utilizing all memory but makes performance less predictable. Local allocation provides predictability but may not use memory efficiently.

mcqs.question: A process experiencing high page fault frequency indicates:

options.text: It has too many frames allocated
It may need more frames to fit its working set
The disk is not being used

mcqs.explanation: High page fault frequency typically indicates the process does not have enough frames to hold its working set, and it needs more frames to reduce faults.
